data.text: Let me read the plan file referenced in the bead description.

data.output:
1	# Artifact Generalization & Three-Layer Run Architecture
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Approved (refined through 3 review rounds with codex + fresh-eyes)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	Two threads converge in this plan:
9	
10	1. **Generalize artifacts beyond markdown documents.** Diagrams, wireframes, hero images, prompts — text and binary — become first-class artifacts via media-type plurality and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files. Generated artifacts are checked into git; regeneration is source-hash-driven.
11	2. **Three-layer run architecture.** DDx owns three explicit layered primitives: `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). The current two-class storage split (`.ddx/exec-runs/` vs `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`) collapses into one substrate.
12	
13	These threads converge: **generating an artifact is a layer-1 agent run** with `produces_artifact` metadata.
14	
15	## Decisions (resolved through review)
16	
17	- **Artifact authority:** identity present → artifact. No "managed asset" second-class concept.
18	- **FEAT-019 fate:** child of FEAT-010 (not peer). Contracts to evaluation UX; workflow shapes (comparison, replay, benchmark) move to skills library.
19	- **Server posture:** 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP for all DDx state, sequenced by impact. Writes case-by-case as workflows demand. Only `artifactRegenerate` write surface added in this plan.
20	- **Generated artifacts:** committed to git unconditionally. Size guard (50MB warn / 100MB hard error) with actionable git-lfs guidance; no auto-init / auto-`.gitattributes`.
21	- **CONTRACT-003:** amendments allowed if FEAT-006 work surfaces gaps. The mountable Cobra root export (below) is a load-bearing audit item.
22	- **Run substrate:** single on-disk shape; layer-2 and layer-3 add metadata. `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse.
23	- **No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.** Comparison, replay, benchmark, etc. are skill compositions, never enshrined in Go core or specs.
24	- **Loop ownership sharpened:** DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory.
25	- **CLI verbs:** `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` promoted to top-level. Hard-deprecate `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop` (exit non-zero with bare redirect message — no aliases, no grace period).
26	- **Generate-artifact layer placement:** pure-return generators (agent returns bytes/text, DDx writes file) are layer-1. Repo-editing generators are layer-2 with bead-less worktree.
27	- **Quorum dispatch:** moves to skill composition. `--quorum` flag goes away; replaced by `compare-prompts` skill.
28	- **`ddx agent` passthrough is structural:** DDx imports the upstream agent module's Cobra root and mounts it under `ddx agent`. Notionally `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))`. DDx does not enumerate per-subcommand wrappers; the entire upstream tree is the source of truth.
29	
30	## Three-layer architecture
31	
32	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
33	|---|---|---|---|---|
34	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + agent config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream `ddx-agent` per CONTRACT-003 |
35	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + agent-run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
36	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
37	
38	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
39	
40	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 if the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata.
41	
42	## Frame artifact updates
43	
44	### docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md
45	- Thesis: "documents AI agents consume" → "artifacts agents produce and consume."
46	- Artifact-management bullet: non-document media + generators.
47	- One sentence on producer/consumer space (no separate matrix).
48	- New Design-Philosophy subsection: **Three-layer run architecture** — `run` / `try` / `work` named as DDx's loop ownership.
49	- KVPs: "Multi-media artifact graph"; "Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)"; note invocation is upstream.
50	- Principle #1: "Artifacts are the product (documents primary)."
51	
52	### docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md
53	- Summary: artifacts (multi-media); three-layer run architecture; one substrate.
54	- Problem: add "no provenance for generated artifacts."
55	- Goals: reword #1; add three-layer-architecture goal; add generated-artifact-regeneration goal; add "100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP."
56	- Non-goals: sharpen loop wording (mechanical drain in scope; supervisory content-aware decisions out). Add: "DDx does not catalog run types beyond the three layers."
57	- FEAT-001/005/006/007/008/010/019/021 blurbs updated.
58	
59	### FEAT-001-cli.md
60	- Promote `run` / `try` / `work` to top-level.
61	- `ddx agent` mounts upstream Cobra root structurally; no DDx-defined leaf subcommands beneath it.
62	- Hard-deprecation handlers for `ddx agent {run, execute-bead, execute-loop}`.
63	- New namespaces: `ddx runs` (cross-layer evidence introspection), `ddx tries` (layer-2 specifically), `ddx work workers` (layer-3 worker management).
64	- Remove FEAT-001:92 backward-compatibility clause for `ddx agent execute-loop`.
65	
66	### FEAT-005-artifacts.md
67	- Identity broadens to non-markdown via sidecar `.ddx.yaml`.
68	- Add `media_type` and `generated_by` fields.
69	- Authority rule documented: identity present → artifact.
70	- Terminology cleanup ("identity," not "ddx: identity").
71	
72	### FEAT-006-agent-service.md
73	- Tighten to layer-1 consumer-side wrapper that powers `ddx run`.
74	- `ddx agent` is structural passthrough (mount upstream Cobra root).
75	- Non-bead Profile/Permissions selection (artifact-keyed path).
76	- Migration table: what stays in FEAT-006 (CONTRACT-003 boundary, profile/permissions), what moves to FEAT-010 (worktree, merge/preserve, evidence bundles, queue drain), what moves to FEAT-001 (CLI surface).
77	- Session-log boundary clarified: DDx owns the envelope/pointer in evidence bundles; upstream owns inner log shape.
78	
79	### FEAT-010-executions.md (substantial refactor)
80	- Three-layer architecture explicit and load-bearing.
81	- Substrate unification: one record shape; layer metadata.
82	- Migrate `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` to one layout.
83	- Specify `ddx work` no-progress / stop conditions.
84	- Narrow read-only amendment for `artifactRegenerate` only.
85	- No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.
86	
87	### FEAT-007-doc-graph.md
88	- Sidecar scanner refactor; `media_type`; `generated_by` edge with separate staleness rule (provenance, not authority — does not cascade like `depends_on`); version bump on Complete-status feature.
89	- 100% read endpoints for graph state, generated-artifact metadata, sidecar content.
90	
91	### FEAT-008-web-ui.md
92	- Media-type-aware rendering (markdown, mermaid SVG, excalidraw embed, image inline, PDF embed).
93	- Regenerate action wired to `artifactRegenerate` mutation; reconcile with existing FEAT-008:391 weak Re-run requirement.
94	- Layer-aware run views (`work` → `try` → `run` drill-down).
95	
96	### FEAT-019-agent-evaluation.md
97	- Contract to evaluation UX (comparison views, grading rubric storage and display, benchmark result aggregation).
98	- Workflow shapes → skills library.
99	- Remove peer-to-FEAT-010 assertions (FEAT-019:40, :404).
100	
101	### FEAT-021-dashboard-ui.md
102	- Layer-aware run-history routes against unified substrate.
103	
104	### FEAT-014, FEAT-013
105	- One-sentence touches: cost attribution and concurrency for generate-artifact.
106	
107	## `ddx agent` subcommand fates
108	
109	`ddx agent` becomes a structural mount of the upstream agent CLI Cobra root. Per-file fate in DDx:
110	
111	**Delete with prejudice (upstream owns these via mounted subtree):**
112	- `agent_list.go`
113	- `agent_capabilities.go`
114	- `agent_doctor.go`
115	- `agent_check.go`
116	- `agent_models.go`
117	- `agent_providers.go`
118	- `agent_route_status.go`
119	- `agent_catalog.go`
120	- `agent_reindex.go`
121	- `agent_benchmark.go` (replaced by `benchmark-suite` skill)
122	- `agent_replay.go` (replaced by `replay-bead` skill)
123	
124	**Move to other DDx namespaces (DDx-only concerns):**
125	- `agent_executions.go` → `tries_*.go`
126	- `agent_log.go` → `runs_log.go`
127	- `agent_metrics.go` → `runs_metrics.go`
128	- `agent_usage.go` → folded into `runs_metrics.go`
129	- `agent_workers.go` → `work_workers.go`
130	
131	**DDx-side overrides on the mounted subtree:**
132	- Deprecation handlers for `agent run` / `agent execute-bead` / `agent execute-loop`.
133	- `agent condense` (DDx-specific output filter — keep as override or move upstream during impl pass).
134	
135	**`agent_cmd.go` itself thins to:** mount upstream Cobra root + register DDx overrides.
136	
137	## Skill library (DDx-shipped workflows)
138	
139	Per FEAT-011's path model: source at `skills/ddx/`, embedded at `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, init-time copies to `.claude/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.
140	
141	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation (replaces `--quorum` flag and `agent benchmark`)
142	- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions, baseline diff (replaces `agent replay`)
143	- `benchmark-suite` — compare across prompt matrix
144	- `adversarial-review`
145	- `effort-estimate`
146	- `bead-breakdown`
147	
148	## Bead breakdown
149	
150	**Frame updates (sequential where dependencies require):**
151	
152	1. Update product-vision.md (+ three-layer subsection)
153	2. Update prd.md (+ three-layer goal, sharpened loop non-goal, no-catalog non-goal)
154	3. Update FEAT-001 (CLI surface refactor; remove backcompat clause; new namespaces) — **hard predecessor for #6, #7**
155	4. Update FEAT-005 (multi-media identity refactor)
156	5. CONTRACT-003 audit — confirm `ddx-agent` exports a mountable Cobra root and other gaps
157	6. *(Conditional)* Open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent` if #5 surfaces gaps
158	7. Update FEAT-006 (layer-1 consumer; structural passthrough; non-bead Profile selection; migration table; session-log envelope boundary)
159	8. Refactor FEAT-010 (three-layer architecture; substrate unification; on-disk migration; no-progress stop conditions; narrow `artifactRegenerate` amendment)
160	9. Update FEAT-007 (sidecar refactor; media_type; generated_by; separate staleness; version bump)
161	10. Contract FEAT-019 to evaluation UX
162	11. Update FEAT-008 (media rendering, regenerate action, layer-aware run views)
163	12. Update FEAT-021 (layer-aware run-history routes)
164	13. Update FEAT-014 (cost attribution for generate-artifact)
165	14. Update FEAT-013 (concurrency note for regenerate)
166	15. **Repo-wide audit & verb migration** — atomic with #3:
167	    - Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands (list above).
168	    - Migrate DDx-only run/evidence subcommands to `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`.
169	    - Register DDx overrides on the mounted subtree (deprecations + condense).
170	    - Delete `agent_reindex.go` outright.
171	    - Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to new verbs.
172	
173	**Design artifacts (concurrent with frame):**
174	
175	16. Architecture: artifact identity schema (concurrent with #4)
176	17. Architecture: graph schema with media_type + generated_by + separate staleness (concurrent with #9)
177	18. Architecture: three-layer run substrate — record shape, layer metadata, on-disk layout, migration, `ddx work` stop conditions (concurrent with #8)
178	19. Sidecar `.ddx.yaml` schema design
179	
180	**Read-coverage thread:**
181	
182	20. Read-coverage audit — enumerate every CLI-visible read; map to HTTP/MCP coverage; identify gaps
183	21. Per-gap beads — sequenced by user-visibility impact, land case-by-case (this is a pointer to ongoing work, not a single bead)
184	
185	**Skill library:**
186	
187	22. `compare-prompts` skill
188	23. `replay-bead` skill
189	24. `benchmark-suite` skill
190	25. `adversarial-review` skill
191	26. `effort-estimate` skill
192	27. `bead-breakdown` skill
193	
194	**Implementation (P2, after frame & design):**
195	
196	- CLI: `ddx run` / `ddx try` top-level commands; mount upstream agent Cobra root; deprecation handlers
197	- CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)
198	- CLI: `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`, `ddx artifact list --media-type <t>`
199	- Storage: sidecar reader/writer; size-guard threshold/warning/tests
200	- Graph: `generated_by` edges + provenance staleness rule
201	- Run framework: refactor to one substrate per #18; generator metadata; `ddx work` no-progress detection
202	- Server: `artifactRegenerate` write endpoint
203	- Server UI: media-type renderers; regenerate action; layer-aware run history
204	- Migration: passive `media_type` only (read-time inference from extension); on-disk run-record migration per #18
205	
206	## Constraints
207	
208	- Beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — old verbs are referenced in scripts/library/personas/init help text/CI; an isolated #3 break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced this plan.
209	- Bead #5 (CONTRACT-003 audit) gates #7 (FEAT-006). #6 conditional.
210	- Architecture sketches (#16–#19) run concurrent with their corresponding frame beads, not after — keeps frame/design from drifting.
211	- Read-coverage epic (#20–#21) is independent and not gated on this plan.
212	
213	## Refinement provenance
214	
215	Refined through 3 rounds of adversarial review (codex via `ddx agent run --harness codex` + fresh-eyes general-purpose subagent in parallel) plus user-resolved questions Q1–Q12. Key user decisions: artifact authority (Q1), FEAT-019 child-of-010 (Q2), reads-matter-writes-case-by-case (Q3), check-in unconditionally (Q4), CONTRACT-003 amendments allowed (Q5), substrate unification (Q6), no run-type catalog (Q7), no v1-gate framing (Q8), FEAT-019 contracts (Q9), generate-artifact split layer-1/layer-2 (Q10), quorum-as-skill (Q11), structural passthrough not per-command (Q12 follow-up).

data.text: Let me read FEAT-005 and an example technical design for format reference.

data.output:
1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: FEAT-005
4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	    - FEAT-007
7	---
8	# Feature: Artifact Convention
9	
10	**ID:** FEAT-005
11	**Status:** In Progress
12	**Priority:** P1
13	**Owner:** DDx Team
14	
15	## Overview
16	
17	An **artifact** is any markdown document with `ddx:` YAML frontmatter. The `ddx.id` field identifies it; the ID prefix conventionally indicates the type (ADR-001, SD-003, MET-007, FEAT-012, etc.). DDx does not hardcode artifact types — it manages the document graph generically. Types are conventions that workflows define.
18	
19	This replaces the previous approach of dedicated `ddx adr` and `ddx sd` commands. Those are removed in favor of the generic `ddx doc` commands (FEAT-007).
20	
21	## Definition
22	
23	An artifact is a markdown file containing:
24	
25	```yaml
26	---
27	ddx:
28	  id: <PREFIX>-<NNN>
29	  depends_on: [<other-ids>]
30	---
31	# Content
32	```
33	
34	That's it. Any file with a `ddx:` block and an `id` field is an artifact. DDx discovers them by scanning for frontmatter, not by looking in specific directories.
35	
36	### Common Artifact Types (by convention)
37	
38	| Prefix | Type | Typical Location |
39	|--------|------|-----------------|
40	| `FEAT` | Feature specification | `docs/helix/01-frame/features/` |
41	| `ADR` | Architecture Decision Record | `docs/adr/` or `docs/helix/02-design/adr/` |
42	| `SD` | Solution Design | `docs/designs/` or `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/` |
43	| `TD` | Technical Design | `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/` |
44	| `TP` | Test Plan | `docs/helix/03-test/` |
45	| `MET` | Metric definition | `docs/metrics/` |
46	| `US` | User Story | `docs/helix/01-frame/user-stories/` |
47	
48	These prefixes are conventions — DDx treats them all the same. Workflows
49	(HELIX) may enforce that certain types exist or follow specific templates.
50	
51	Projects may introduce additional conventions such as `AC-*` for acceptance
52	criteria or `TC-*` for test cases. DDx does not privilege those types; it only
53	tracks their IDs and relationships.
54	
55	### Frontmatter Schema
56	
57	| Field | Type | Required | Description |
58	|-------|------|----------|-------------|
59	| `id` | string | yes | Unique identifier with type prefix (e.g., `ADR-001`) |
60	| `depends_on` | []string | no | IDs of artifacts this one depends on |
61	| `inputs` | []string | no | Input selectors for prompt context resolution |
62	| `review` | object | no | Staleness tracking (managed by `ddx doc stamp`) |
63	| `prompt` | string | no | Custom prompt for updating this document |
64	| `parking_lot` | bool | no | Skip in staleness checks |
65	
66	Unknown fields are preserved on round-trip.
67	
68	## The Artifact Graph
69	
70	All artifacts in a project form a **single directed acyclic graph** via `depends_on` edges. This graph is the project's authority structure — it encodes which documents govern which.
71	
72	```
73	Product Vision
74	  └─ PRD
75	       ├─ FEAT-001
76	       │    ├─ SD-001
77	       │    │    └─ TD-001
78	       │    │         └─ TP-001
79	       │    └─ US-001
80	       ├─ FEAT-002
81	       │    └─ ADR-001
82	       └─ MET-001
83	```
84	
85	Every artifact should be reachable from a root (typically the vision or PRD). Orphaned artifacts — those with no incoming or outgoing edges — are a smell that `ddx doc validate` flags.
86	
87	### Graph Properties
88	
89	- **Single graph per project.** All artifacts with `ddx:` frontmatter participate in one graph. There are no separate "ADR graph" or "feature graph" partitions.
90	- **Edges are explicit.** Only `depends_on` creates edges. No implicit edges from directory structure or naming.
91	- **Direction is authority.** An edge from B to A means "A governs B" — if A changes, B may be stale.
92	- **Staleness cascades.** If a node is stale, all its descendants are transitively stale.
93	- **Types are orthogonal.** The graph doesn't care about artifact types. An ADR can depend on a feature spec; a metric can depend on a PRD; a test plan can depend on a solution design. The graph is type-agnostic.
94	
95	## What DDx Does With Artifacts
96	
97	All via `ddx doc` commands (FEAT-007):
98	
99	1. **Discover** — scan for `ddx:` frontmatter in markdown files
100	2. **Graph** — build the single project-wide dependency DAG
101	3. **Stale** — detect when upstream dependencies changed since last stamp
102	4. **Stamp** — record review hashes after updating a document
103	5. **List** — list artifacts with optional type filtering (`ddx doc list --type ADR`)
104	6. **Show** — display artifact content and metadata
105	7. **Validate** — check frontmatter structure, dependency references exist, no circular deps, no orphans
106	
107	## What DDx Does NOT Do
108	
109	- **Scaffold from templates** — agents and workflows create documents. DDx doesn't need `create` commands for each type.
110	- **Enforce type-specific structure** — "ADRs must have a Decision section" is a workflow concern. DDx validates the graph; dun validates content structure.
111	- **Hardcode artifact types** — no switch statements on type prefixes. The graph treats all artifacts equally.
112	- **Partition the graph** — all artifacts are in one graph regardless of type or directory.
113	- **Execute artifact documents directly** — runtime execution belongs to DDx executions (FEAT-010), not the artifact graph itself.
114	
115	## Templates and Prompts
116	
117	Each artifact type has associated resources in the document library:
118	
119	```
120	.ddx/library/artifacts/<type>/
121	├── template.md       # Structural template (frontmatter + sections)
122	├── create.md         # Prompt for creating a new instance
123	├── evolve.md         # Prompt for updating when dependencies change
124	└── check.md          # Prompt for reviewing/validating the artifact
125	```
126	
127	For example, `.ddx/library/artifacts/adr/`:
128	- `template.md` — ADR skeleton with Context/Decision/Alternatives sections
129	- `create.md` — prompt instructing an agent how to write a good ADR
130	- `evolve.md` — prompt for updating an ADR when upstream requirements change
131	- `check.md` — prompt for reviewing an ADR against its governing artifacts
132	
133	These are **library resources**, not CLI features. Agents and workflows read them from the library when operating on artifacts. DDx ships defaults; projects can override with project-specific versions.
134	
135	The doc graph (FEAT-007) can resolve these prompts via input selectors:
136	- `artifact-prompt:ADR:create` → the create prompt for ADR artifacts
137	- `artifact-template:ADR` → the template for ADR artifacts
138	
139	## Artifacts and Executions
140	
141	Artifacts stay declarative. They define meaning, authority, and relationships
142	in the document graph. DDx does **not** execute artifact files directly.
143	
144	Runtime evaluation belongs to DDx execution definitions and execution runs
145	(FEAT-010), which are file-backed runtime records linked to artifacts by ID.
146	
147	### Boundary
148	
149	- Artifacts declare what matters, who governs it, and how it relates to other
150	  artifacts in the graph.
151	- Execution definitions describe how to evaluate, verify, or measure one or
152	  more artifacts.
153	- Execution runs capture one immutable invocation: raw logs, structured result
154	  data, status, and provenance.
155	- Execution definitions and runs are not artifacts, and they do not
156	  participate in document staleness or graph validation.
157	- DDx does not infer execution semantics from an artifact type prefix.
158	
159	### Examples
160	
161	- A `MET-*` artifact may link to an execution definition that emits numeric
162	  observations over time.
163	- Metric comparison and trend views are projections over those execution runs,
164	  not evidence stored in a separate metric-owned runtime backend.
165	- A project may model acceptance criteria or test cases as standalone artifacts
166	  and link them to execution definitions that emit pass/fail or richer
167	  structured results.
168	- An execution definition may evaluate multiple governing artifacts together as
169	  long as those links are explicit in the runtime record.
170	
171	The shared rule is simple: artifact IDs establish meaning and authority; DDx
172	executions establish repeatable runtime behavior and evidence.
173	
174	## Migration
175	
176	The dedicated `ddx adr` and `ddx sd` commands are removed. Their functionality is subsumed by:
177	
178	| Old Command | Replacement |
179	|-------------|-------------|
180	| `ddx adr create` | Agent creates file from template |
181	| `ddx adr list` | `ddx doc list --type ADR` |
182	| `ddx adr show ADR-001` | `ddx doc show ADR-001` |
183	| `ddx adr validate` | `ddx doc validate` |
184	| `ddx sd create` | Agent creates file from template |
185	| `ddx sd list` | `ddx doc list --type SD` |
186	| `ddx sd show SD-001` | `ddx doc show SD-001` |
187	| `ddx sd validate` | `ddx doc validate` |
188	
189	## Dependencies
190	
191	- FEAT-007 (Doc Graph) — provides the `ddx doc` commands
192	- Document library with templates
193	
194	## Out of Scope
195	
196	- Type-specific CLI commands (use `ddx doc` generically)
197	- Content structure validation (that's dun/workflow-level)
198	- Template application commands (agents do this)
199

2	ddx:
3	  id: TD-010
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-006
6	    - FEAT-010
7	    - ADR-004
8	---
9	# Technical Design: Execution Definitions and Runs
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This design defines the generic DDx execution substrate introduced by
14	FEAT-010. It establishes the storage model, collection boundaries, attachment
15	layout, compatibility rules, and inspection semantics that all execution
16	specializations must reuse.
17	
18	Metric- or acceptance-specific behavior is layered on top of this design. The
19	generic substrate owns:
20	
21	- execution-definition storage
22	- immutable execution-run storage
23	- attachment publication
24	- read/write compatibility with legacy exec layouts
25	- CLI and server inspection behavior for definitions, runs, logs, and results
26	
27	## Definition Sources
28	
29	Execution definitions have two authoritative sources:
30	
31	1. **Graph-authored execution documents** — git-tracked markdown files with
32	   `ddx:` frontmatter (see FEAT-007). These are the preferred source when using
33	   `ddx agent execute-bead`. DDx discovers them by traversing the document graph
34	   from the target bead and its governing artifacts. They participate in ordinary
35	   document indexing and staleness tracking.
36	
37	2. **Runtime-managed definitions** — machine-readable records stored in the
38	   `exec-definitions` collection. These are valid for `ddx exec` operations that
39	   do not require graph discovery.
40	
41	DDx may maintain an internal indexed or compiled representation of graph-authored
42	execution documents for runtime speed, but the git-tracked document is the source
43	of truth. `ddx exec validate/run/history/result/log` resolve and operate on both
44	sources; graph-authored definitions take precedence when both exist for the same
45	artifact.
46	
47	### Acceptance Criteria (definition source priority and metric projection)
48	
49	- Given a graph-authored execution document and a runtime-managed definition in `exec-definitions` both exist for the same artifact ID, when `ddx exec validate` or `ddx exec run` resolves that artifact, then the graph-authored document is used as the authoritative definition.
50	- Given only a runtime-managed definition exists for an artifact, when `ddx exec run` is invoked, then it proceeds using the runtime-managed definition without error.
51	- Given DDx processes execution results from a run, when `ddx metric` is queried for the resulting metric, then no `.ddx/metrics/` directory is created — results are served entirely from `exec-runs` collection records.
52	
53	## Storage Model
54	
55	Executions use named bead-backed collections rather than a bespoke `.ddx/exec/`
56	metadata format.
57	
58	- `exec-definitions` stores machine-readable runtime-managed execution definitions
59	- `exec-runs` stores immutable execution-run metadata rows
60	- `exec-runs.d/` stores attachment-backed payloads for one run
61	
62	For the JSONL backend, those logical collections map to:
63	
64	```text
65	.ddx/
66	├── exec-definitions.jsonl
67	├── exec-runs.jsonl
68	└── exec-runs.d/
69	    └── <run-id>/
70	        ├── result.json
71	        ├── stdout.log
72	        └── stderr.log
73	```
74	
75	Other backends preserve the same logical collection boundary without DDx
76	prescribing their physical layout.
77	
78	Legacy compatibility:
79	
80	- older definition bundles under `.ddx/exec/definitions/*.json` remain readable
81	  during migration
82	- older run bundles under `.ddx/exec/runs/<run-id>/` remain readable during
83	  migration
84	- new authoritative writes target the bead-backed collections and attachment
85	  directories above
86	
87	This storage model does not own the tracked `execute-bead` attempt bundle under
88	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`.
89	
90	- `exec-runs` / `exec-runs.d` remain the generic runtime substrate for
91	  execution definitions and reusable run history
92	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` is a tracked git artifact for one
93	  `execute-bead` attempt, containing prompt/manifest/result/check provenance
94	  for implementation replay and audit
95	
96	If an `execute-bead` iteration runs graph-authored execution definitions, it
97	may reference `exec-runs` records from its bundle, but it must not collapse the
98	two storage classes into one path or retention policy.
99	
100	## Pre-exec Metric Storage Migration
101	
102	Prior to the exec substrate (before commit `2647ae4`), the metric store wrote
103	directly to:
104	
105	```text
106	.ddx/
107	└── metrics/
108	    ├── definitions/
109	    │   └── <definition-id>.json    # one JSON file per definition
110	    └── history.jsonl               # append-only JSONL history log
111	```
112	
113	This layout was only present in very early DDx builds before the exec substrate
114	was introduced. It was never part of a public or tagged release.
115	
116	Migration status: **no migration is required.**
117	
118	The current metric store delegates entirely to the exec substrate. It does not
119	read from `.ddx/metrics/` at all. If an old `.ddx/metrics/` directory is
120	present, DDx ignores it silently — no crash, no data corruption, no stale reads.
121	
122	Operators who upgraded from an early pre-release build and want to reclaim disk
123	space may safely delete `.ddx/metrics/`. Historical metric runs stored there
124	will no longer appear in `ddx metric history`, but this data loss is acceptable
125	given that no public release ever wrote to that path.
126	
127	## Definition Record Shape
128	
129	Each definition is stored as one bead-backed row in the `exec-definitions`
130	collection. The shared bead envelope does not gain new top-level fields for
131	execution; the definition payload lives in preserved extras.
132	
133	```json
134	{
135	  "id": "exec-metric-startup-time@1",
136	  "issue_type": "exec_definition",
137	  "status": "open",
138	  "title": "Execution definition for MET-001",
139	  "labels": ["artifact:MET-001", "executor:command"],
140	  "definition": {
141	    "id": "exec-metric-startup-time@1",
142	    "artifact_ids": ["MET-001"],
143	    "executor": {
144	      "kind": "command",
145	      "command": ["go", "test", "./..."],
146	      "cwd": ".",
147	      "timeout_ms": 30000
148	    },
149	    "result": {
150	      "metric": {
151	        "unit": "ms",
152	        "value_path": "$.duration_ms"
153	      }
154	    },
155	    "evaluation": {
156	      "comparison": "lower-is-better"
157	    },
158	    "active": true,
159	    "created_at": "2026-04-04T15:00:00Z"
160	  }
161	}
162	```
163	
164	Rules:
165	
166	- the bead envelope provides shared runtime metadata and backend portability
167	- the full execution definition is stored inside preserved extras under
168	  `definition`
169	- `status=open` means active; `status=closed` means retired but preserved
170	- one execution definition may link to one or more artifact IDs
171	- collection readers must ignore retired or malformed definitions rather than
172	  inventing partial runtime contracts
173	
174	## Run Record Shape
175	
176	Each execution produces one bead-backed row in the `exec-runs` collection plus
177	zero or more sidecar attachments. The shared bead envelope does not gain new
178	top-level fields for execution; run payloads live in preserved extras.
179	
180	```json
181	{
182	  "id": "exec-metric-startup-time@2026-04-04T15:01:00Z-1",
183	  "issue_type": "exec_run",
184	  "status": "closed",
185	  "title": "Execution run for MET-001",
186	  "labels": ["artifact:MET-001", "executor:command"],
187	  "run": {
188	    "run_id": "exec-metric-startup-time@2026-04-04T15:01:00Z-1",
189	    "definition_id": "exec-metric-startup-time@1",
190	    "artifact_ids": ["MET-001"],
191	    "started_at": "2026-04-04T15:01:00Z",
192	    "finished_at": "2026-04-04T15:01:01Z",
193	    "status": "success",
194	    "exit_code": 0,
195	    "attachments": {
196	      "stdout": "exec-runs.d/exec-metric-startup-time@2026-04-04T15:01:00Z-1/stdout.log",
197	      "stderr": "exec-runs.d/exec-metric-startup-time@2026-04-04T15:01:00Z-1/stderr.log",
198	      "result": "exec-runs.d/exec-metric-startup-time@2026-04-04T15:01:00Z-1/result.json"
199	    },
200	    "provenance": {
201	      "git_rev": "abc123",
202	      "ddx_version": "0.1.0"
203	    }
204	  },
205	  "result": {
206	    "metric": {
207	      "artifact_id": "MET-001",
208	      "definition_id": "exec-metric-startup-time@1",
209	      "observed_at": "2026-04-04T15:01:00Z",
210	      "status": "pass",
211	      "value": 14.6,
212	      "unit": "ms",
213	      "samples": [14.6]
214	    }
215	  }
216	}
217	```
218	
219	Rules:
220	
221	- the bead envelope `status` tracks row lifecycle, not domain outcome
222	- domain execution outcome lives in `run.status`
223	- the full structured result payload may appear inline under `result` and may
224	  also be published to `attachments.result` for durable inspection
225	- attachment references are relative, repo-local paths stored in preserved
226	  extras
227	- run rows are append-only; later executions never rewrite prior run rows
228	
229	## Attachment Publication
230	
231	Large bodies are written outside the collection row:
232	
233	- stdout
234	- stderr
235	- normalized result payload
236	- future executor-specific payloads
237	
238	Write order:
239	
240	1. Resolve the target collection and attachment root.
241	2. Create the payloads in a temporary attachment directory.
242	3. Acquire the collection lock.
243	4. Copy the current collection snapshot to a backup when repair or rewrite
244	   safety is required.
245	5. Publish the attachment directory into its final location with an atomic
246	   rename or backend-equivalent swap.
247	6. Append or update the bead-backed metadata row in the target collection.
248	7. Release the lock.
249	
250	This guarantees published rows do not point at missing attachment paths. A
251	crash may leave an orphan temporary directory or backup copy, but it must not
252	leave a partial published row.
253	
254	## Read and Inspection Semantics
255	
256	The generic execution substrate owns these inspection paths:
257	
258	- `ddx exec list [--artifact ID]`
259	- `ddx exec show <definition-id>`
260	- `ddx exec validate <definition-id>`
261	- `ddx exec run <definition-id>`
262	- `ddx exec history [--artifact ID] [--definition ID]`
263	- `ddx exec log <run-id>`
264	- `ddx exec result <run-id>`
265	
266	Read rules:
267	
268	- list/show/history prefer bead-backed collection records
269	- legacy `.ddx/exec/` data remains a read fallback during migration
270	- detail readers resolve attachments lazily from the referenced paths
271	- sorting is deterministic:
272	  - definitions by `created_at` descending, then `id`
273	  - runs by `started_at` ascending, then `run_id`
274	
275	Server read APIs must use the same underlying reader logic so CLI and HTTP/MCP
276	do not drift.
277	
278	## Validation Algorithm
279	
280	`ddx exec validate <definition-id>` performs:
281	
282	1. Load the preferred definition from the collection, falling back to legacy
283	   storage only when no collection record exists.
284	2. Verify the definition has an ID and at least one linked artifact ID.
285	3. Resolve each linked artifact ID through the document graph.
286	4. Validate the executor kind and executor-specific fields.
287	5. Validate any specialization-specific result/evaluation fields.
288	6. Report the failing definition ID, artifact ID, and storage source
289	   deterministically.
290	
291	## Run Algorithm
292	
293	`ddx exec run <definition-id>` performs:
294	
295	1. Validate the definition and linked artifacts.
296	2. Invoke the configured executor.
297	3. Capture stdout, stderr, exit code, timestamps, and provenance.
298	4. Normalize the structured result payload.
299	5. Publish attachments.
300	6. Persist one immutable `exec-runs` metadata row.
301	
302	The first implementation supports `command` execution. `agent` remains a
303	defined executor kind and must eventually delegate to `ddx agent` while
304	preserving the same run/attachment model.
305	
306	## Failure Modes
307	
308	- missing linked artifact: validation fails with the missing artifact ID
309	- unknown executor kind: validation fails before invocation
310	- attachment publish failure: no metadata row is published
311	- malformed collection record: the reader reports the concrete collection and
312	  record ID
313	- corrupt legacy run bundle: the reader reports the concrete bundle path
314	
315	## Execution Bundle Archive (Mirror)
316	
317	`exec-runs` / `exec-runs.d/` (above) own the generic exec substrate. The
318	parallel `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` tree owns one `execute-bead` attempt
319	bundle. The bundle's most valuable component — the per-iteration agent trace at
320	`embedded/agent-*.jsonl` — cannot reasonably be checked in: a single trace can
321	exceed 50 MB and a busy automation day produces hundreds of MB. axon hit the
322	GitHub 50 MB warning on 2026-04-15, and `embedded/` is now gitignored
323	per-repo.
324	
325	To preserve forensic detail without inflating git history, DDx supports an
326	out-of-band mirror of the full bundle.
327	
328	### Configuration
329	
330	Configured under `.ddx/config.yaml`:
331	
332	```yaml
333	executions:
334	  mirror:
335	    kind: local                                  # local | s3 | gcs | http
336	    path: /var/lib/ddx-mirror/{project}/{attempt_id}
337	    include: [manifest, prompt, result, usage, checks, embedded]
338	    async: true
339	  retain_days: 30
340	```
341	
342	`path` supports the placeholders `{project}`, `{attempt_id}`, `{date}`,
343	`{bead_id}`. Unknown placeholders pass through unchanged. `{date}` resolves
344	to the bundle's UTC date as `YYYY-MM-DD`, derived from the attempt id when
345	the id begins with the standard `YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS-` prefix and falling back
346	to the current UTC date otherwise.
347	
348	`include` defaults to the full bundle when absent. Operators may exclude
349	`embedded` for bandwidth-sensitive setups while keeping the small
350	checked-in artifacts mirrored.
351	
352	`async` defaults to `true`. The mirror runs in a background goroutine after
353	result.json is written so it never blocks the bead's hot path.
354	
355	Currently only `kind: local` is implemented. Other kinds (s3, gcs, http) are
356	defined in the config schema but the backend constructor returns a clear
357	"unsupported mirror kind" error when selected. Adding a new backend means
358	implementing `agent.MirrorBackend` and wiring it into `agent.NewMirrorBackend`.
359	
360	### When mirroring runs
361	
362	The hook fires inside `agent.ExecuteBead` immediately after the worker writes
363	the final `result.json`. At that point the bundle directory contains
364	`manifest.json`, `prompt.md`, `result.json`, optionally `usage.json`,
365	optionally `checks.json`, and the entire `embedded/` directory of agent
366	traces.
367	
368	Mirror failures **never** affect the bead outcome:
369	
370	- failures are recorded one line per attempt to `.ddx/agent-logs/mirror.log`
371	- the bead's `result.json` is unchanged
372	- async mode swallows the error inside the goroutine; sync mode logs and
373	  returns
374	
375	### Mirror index
376	
377	Each successful upload appends one JSON object per line to
378	`.ddx/executions/mirror-index.jsonl`:
379	
380	```json
381	{"attempt_id":"20260418T061717-1993d293","bead_id":"ddx-5930ed71","mirror_uri":"/var/lib/ddx-mirror/ddx/20260418T061717-1993d293","uploaded_at":"2026-04-18T06:17:18Z","byte_size":54123890,"kind":"local"}
382	```
383	
384	The index is local-only — the mirror itself is the durable store. The index
385	exists so analysts can locate a bundle's mirror URI with one `jq` filter
386	without walking the remote backend.
387	
388	### Retrieval
389	
390	`ddx agent executions fetch <attempt-id>` resolves the index entry, dispatches
391	to the matching backend's `Fetch`, and rehydrates the bundle into
392	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` (or `--dest` when given). Subsequent local
393	inspection (replay, review) works unchanged because the on-disk layout matches
394	the original execute-bead write.
395	
396	### Local retention
397	
398	`executions.retain_days` is an optional local-only retention policy. When set,
399	operators may prune local bundles older than the threshold while the mirror
400	keeps the full history. The setting is recorded in config now; the GC
401	implementation is left to a follow-up bead. The mirror itself has no DDx-side
402	retention — operators manage retention on the mirror backend.
403	
404	### Operator runbook
405	
406	1. Configure `.ddx/config.yaml` with an `executions.mirror` block pointing at
407	   a writable directory or bucket.
408	2. Run a normal `ddx work` / `ddx agent execute-bead` cycle. Confirm one new
409	   row in `.ddx/executions/mirror-index.jsonl` per finalized attempt.
410	3. To inspect an old bundle locally:
411	   `ddx agent executions fetch <attempt-id>`
412	4. To diagnose a mirror failure:
413	   `tail -F .ddx/agent-logs/mirror.log`
414	5. To exclude the per-iteration trace (large): set
415	   `include: [manifest, prompt, result, usage, checks]` and re-run.
416

7	# Feature: Document Dependency Graph
8	
9	**ID:** FEAT-007
10	**Status:** Complete
11	**Priority:** P0
12	**Owner:** DDx Team
13	
14	## Overview
15	
16	DDx tracks dependencies between documents using YAML frontmatter metadata. When an upstream document changes, DDx detects which downstream documents are stale and need review. This is the "keep documents honest" capability — the infrastructure that prevents document drift.
17	
18	Workflow tools and check runners consume the graph to enforce document quality. DDx owns the graph model, staleness detection, hashing, and stamping. Check runners delegate graph operations to DDx rather than implementing their own.
19	
20	## Problem Statement
21	
22	**Current situation:** Dun implements document dependency tracking internally (`doc_dag.go`, `frontmatter.go`, `hash.go`, `stamp.go`). This is document infrastructure, not check logic — it belongs in DDx alongside the document library it operates on.
23	
24	**Pain points:**
25	- Document staleness detection is locked inside the check runner, inaccessible to other tools
26	- No CLI command to inspect the document graph, check staleness, or stamp documents
27	- No MCP endpoint for agents to query document relationships
28	- The `dun:` frontmatter prefix ties a generic concept to a specific tool
29	
30	**Desired outcome:** DDx owns the document graph as a first-class service. Any tool can query it. The `ddx:` frontmatter convention is the standard way to declare document identity and dependencies.
31	
32	## The `ddx:` Frontmatter Convention
33	
34	Documents declare their identity and dependencies using YAML frontmatter with the `ddx:` namespace. GitHub hides YAML frontmatter in its markdown viewer, so this is invisible to casual readers.
35	
36	### Format
37	
38	```yaml
39	---
40	ddx:
41	  id: helix.architecture
42	  depends_on:
43	    - helix.prd
44	    - helix.feature-registry
45	  inputs:
46	    - node:helix.prd
47	    - refs:helix.prd
48	  review:
49	    self_hash: "a3f2dd..."
50	    deps:
51	      helix.prd: "7c6f43..."
52	    reviewed_at: "2026-04-03T12:00:00Z"
53	---
54	# Architecture Document
55	
56	Content here...
57	```
58	
59	### Fields
60	
61	| Field | Type | Required | Description |
62	|-------|------|----------|-------------|
63	| `id` | string | yes | Unique document identifier (e.g., `helix.prd`, `SD-001`) |
64	| `depends_on` | []string | no | IDs of documents this one depends on |
65	| `inputs` | []string | no | Input selectors for prompt context resolution |
66	| `review` | object | no | Staleness tracking metadata (managed by `ddx doc stamp`) |
67	| `review.self_hash` | string | auto | Content hash of this document (excluding review block) |
68	| `review.deps` | map[string]string | auto | Map of dependency ID → hash at last review |
69	| `review.reviewed_at` | string | auto | ISO-8601 timestamp of last stamp |
70	| `prompt` | string | no | Custom prompt for updating this document |
71	| `parking_lot` | bool | no | Skip this document in staleness checks |
72	
73	### Compatibility
74	
75	- The `ddx:` prefix replaces the existing `dun:` prefix. DDx should read both `ddx:` and `dun:` frontmatter for backward compatibility, but write `ddx:`.
76	- Documents without `ddx:` frontmatter are ignored by the graph.
77	- Unknown fields within the `ddx:` block are preserved on round-trip.
78	
79	## Requirements
80	
81	### Functional
82	
83	1. **Frontmatter parsing** — read `ddx:` (and legacy `dun:`) YAML frontmatter from markdown files
84	2. **Graph construction** — scan a directory tree, parse frontmatter, build a directed acyclic graph of document dependencies
85	3. **Content hashing** — deterministic hash of document content excluding the `review` block. Same content = same hash always.
86	4. **Staleness detection** — a document is stale when any dependency's current hash differs from the hash recorded in `review.deps`
87	5. **Cascade propagation** — if a document is stale, all its dependents are transitively stale
88	6. **Stamp command** (`ddx doc stamp`) — update `review.self_hash` and `review.deps` to mark a document as reviewed against its current dependencies
89	7. **Graph query** (`ddx doc graph`) — show the document dependency graph as text or JSON
90	8. **Stale query** (`ddx doc stale`) — list stale documents
91	9. **Input resolution** — resolve `inputs` selectors (`node:`, `refs:`, `code_refs:`, `paths:`) to actual content for prompt assembly
92	10. **Graph configuration** — optional `.ddx/graphs/*.yaml` files defining required roots, ID-to-path mappings, and cascade rules
93	11. **Body-link indexing** — scan document bodies for `[[ID]]` reference syntax and index those as graph edges alongside frontmatter-declared `depends_on` edges. Support plain IDs (`[[FEAT-001]]`), slugged IDs (`[[US-036-list-mcp-servers]]`), and dotted IDs (`[[helix.workflow.artifact-hierarchy]]`). Return the union of body links and frontmatter edges without duplicate edges.
94	
95	### Non-Functional
96	
97	- **Performance:** Graph construction <500ms for repos with 100+ documents
98	- **Determinism:** Same document content always produces the same hash
99	- **Portability:** Works on any repo with markdown files containing `ddx:` frontmatter
100	- **Backward compatibility:** Reads `dun:` frontmatter, writes `ddx:`
101	
102	## CLI Commands
103	
104	```bash
105	ddx doc graph [--json]              # Show document dependency graph
106	ddx doc stale [--json]              # List stale documents
107	ddx doc stamp [paths...] [--all]    # Update review stamps
108	ddx doc show <id>                   # Show document metadata and status
109	ddx doc deps <id>                   # Show what a document depends on
110	ddx doc dependents <id>             # Show what depends on a document
111	```
112	
113	## Server Endpoints (FEAT-002 integration)
114	
115	| MCP Tool | HTTP Endpoint | Description |
116	|----------|--------------|-------------|
117	| `ddx_doc_graph` | `GET /api/docs/graph` | Full dependency graph |
118	| `ddx_doc_stale` | `GET /api/docs/stale` | List stale documents |
119	| `ddx_doc_show` | `GET /api/docs/:id` | Document metadata and status |
120	| `ddx_doc_deps` | `GET /api/docs/:id/deps` | Dependencies of a document |
121	
122	## User Stories
123	
124	### US-070: Developer Checks Document Freshness
125	**As a** developer maintaining project docs
126	**I want** to see which documents are stale after upstream changes
127	**So that** I know what needs reviewing
128	
129	**Acceptance Criteria:**
130	- Given the PRD has changed since the architecture doc was last stamped, when I run `ddx doc stale`, then the architecture doc is listed as stale
131	- Given I update the architecture doc and run `ddx doc stamp docs/architecture.md`, then it's no longer reported as stale
132	
133	### US-071: Agent Discovers Document Relationships
134	**As an** AI agent working on a feature
135	**I want** to query which documents govern this area of the codebase
136	**So that** I can load the right context
137	
138	**Acceptance Criteria:**
139	- Given ddx-server is running, when an agent calls `ddx_doc_deps` with a design doc ID, then it receives the list of upstream governing documents
140	- Given an agent calls `ddx_doc_stale`, then it knows which documents need attention before proceeding
141	
142	### US-073: Graph Indexes Body Links and Supports Reverse Traversal
143	**As** DDx (and tools consuming the graph)
144	**I want** document body `[[ID]]` references indexed as graph edges
145	**So that** dependency relationships declared in prose are machine-queryable without grep
146	
147	**Acceptance Criteria:**
148	- Given a markdown document containing `[[FEAT-011]]`, `[[US-036-list-mcp-servers]]`, and `[[helix.workflow.artifact-hierarchy]]` in its body, when DDx constructs the graph, then those references are indexed as resolvable graph edges.
149	- Given both `ddx.depends_on: [A]` frontmatter and a `[[A]]` body reference in the same document, when the graph is queried, then a single edge to A is returned — not two.
150	- Given documents B and C each contain `[[A]]` in their bodies, when dependents of A are queried, then B and C are returned from the graph index without requiring an external file scan.
151	- Given an execution document declares `ddx.depends_on: [FEAT-001]`, when the graph is queried, then the execution document is returned as a discoverable graph artifact linked to FEAT-001.
152	- Given malformed `[[...]]` syntax (e.g., `[[]]`, `[[has spaces]]`) or an `[[UnknownID]]` reference, when the graph is constructed, then the reference is skipped silently and graph construction completes without error.
153	
154	### US-072: Check Runner Delegates Graph Operations
155	**As** dun (check runner)
156	**I want** to call `ddx doc stale` instead of implementing my own graph logic
157	**So that** document staleness is a shared service
158	
159	**Acceptance Criteria:**
160	- Given dun's doc-dag check is configured, when it runs, then it calls `ddx doc stale --json` and reports the results
161	- Given dun's change-cascade check detects upstream changes, then it calls `ddx doc stale` to identify affected downstream documents
162	
163	## Implementation Notes
164	
165	### Porting from Dun
166	
167	The following dun source files contain the logic to port:
168	
169	| Dun File | What It Does | DDx Destination |
170	|----------|-------------|----------------|
171	| `internal/dun/frontmatter.go` | Parse/write `dun:` frontmatter | `internal/docgraph/frontmatter.go` |
172	| `internal/dun/doc_dag.go` | Build graph, detect staleness, cascade | `internal/docgraph/graph.go` |
173	| `internal/dun/hash.go` | Deterministic content hashing | `internal/docgraph/hash.go` |
174	| `internal/dun/stamp.go` | Update review stamps in frontmatter | `internal/docgraph/stamp.go` |
175	
176	### Migration Strategy
177	
178	**Phase 1:** DDx ships doc graph commands (`ddx doc graph/stale/stamp`). Reads both `ddx:` and `dun:` frontmatter, writes `ddx:`.
179	
180	**Phase 2:** Dun adds `ddx doc stale --json` as alternative to internal graph logic, gated by `DUN_USE_DDX_DOC=1`.
181	
182	**Phase 3:** Once proven, dun removes internal doc_dag/frontmatter/hash/stamp code and delegates fully to DDx.
183	
184	**Phase 4:** A one-time migration tool converts `dun:` frontmatter to `ddx:` in existing repos.
185	
186	## Edge Cases
187	
188	- Document with `ddx:` frontmatter but no `id` — skip, warn
189	- Circular dependencies — detect and report, don't infinite loop
190	- Missing dependency (ID referenced but document not found) — report as warning, don't fail
191	- Mixed `dun:` and `ddx:` in same repo — read both, prefer `ddx:` if both present on same file
192	- Binary files or non-markdown — skip silently
193	- Very large repos (1000+ markdown files) — incremental scanning with caching
194	
195	## Dependencies
196	
197	- DDx CLI infrastructure (config, command factory)
198	- Markdown files with `ddx:` frontmatter
199	- For server endpoints: FEAT-002
200	
201	## Execution Documents
202	
203	Execution documents are a category of graph-discovered artifact that declare
204	what validations, checks, and measurements should run for a bead or set of
205	governing artifacts. They live in the worktree as ordinary markdown files with
206	`ddx:` frontmatter and participate in DDx document indexing alongside other
207	graph artifacts.
208	
209	### Frontmatter convention
210	
211	```yaml
212	---
213	ddx:
214	  id: exec.FEAT-001.acceptance-smoke
215	  depends_on:
216	    - FEAT-001
217	  execution:
218	    kind: command             # command | agent
219	    required: true            # true = merge-blocking in execute-bead
220	    command: ["make", "test"]
221	    cwd: cli
222	    timeout_ms: 120000
223	---
224	# Acceptance Smoke Test for FEAT-001
225	...
226	```
227	
228	### Key fields
229	
230	| Field | Description |
231	|-------|-------------|
232	| `ddx.id` | Unique document ID; by convention prefixed `exec.` |
233	| `ddx.depends_on` | Governing artifacts this execution is linked to |
234	| `ddx.execution.kind` | Executor kind: `command` or `agent` |
235	| `ddx.execution.required` | `true` means this execution is merge-blocking in `execute-bead` |
236	
237	### Discovery
238	
239	`ddx agent execute-bead` resolves applicable execution documents from the graph
240	inside the execution worktree by following dependency links from the target bead
241	and its governing artifacts. DDx indexes execution documents like other graph
242	artifacts; no separate registry is required.
243	
244	### Relationship to FEAT-010
245	
246	Execution documents are the git-backed, authored source of truth for what
247	`ddx exec` runs. FEAT-010's exec substrate stores immutable run history.
248	FEAT-007's graph owns the discovery and indexing of execution document
249	definitions.
250	
251	## Git-Aware History (FEAT-012 Integration)
252	
253	The following commands extend the doc graph with revision-control-aware
254	queries. They are defined in FEAT-012 but operate on the document graph:
255	
256	```bash
257	ddx doc history <id> [--since <ref>]   # Commit log for an artifact
258	ddx doc diff <id> [<ref1>] [<ref2>]    # Content diff between refs
259	ddx doc changed [--since <ref>]        # Artifacts changed since a ref
260	```
261	
262	These require the graph's artifact-ID-to-file-path mapping and are
263	surfaced alongside the existing graph/stale/stamp commands.
264	
265	## Out of Scope
266	
267	- Code-to-document tracing (which code implements which spec) — that's dun's `spec-binding` check
268	- Automated document updating — DDx detects staleness, agents do the updating
269	- Document content validation (correct sections, completeness) — that's workflow-level
270	- Branch management, remote operations, merge conflict resolution (see FEAT-012 Out of Scope)
271	- **Graph traversal policy** — authority ordering, impact-flow strategy, and search fallback policy are delegated to workflow tools; DDx provides the graph primitives (edges, dependents, staleness), not the traversal strategy
272	- **Artifact decomposition policy** — how to break a change across an artifact stack is a plugin concern; DDx indexes the artifacts and their relationships
273

input.content:
---
ddx:
  id: TD-025
  depends_on:
    - FEAT-005
    - FEAT-007
---
# Technical Design: Sidecar `.ddx.yaml` Schema

## Purpose

This design specifies the sidecar file format that extends DDx artifact identity
to non-markdown files (images, diagrams, PDFs, binary exports, plain-text
prompts, etc.). It covers three decisions the scanner and validator must agree
on:

1. **Pairing rule** — how a sidecar file maps to its paired asset.
2. **Conflict rule** — what happens when a file could claim identity via both
   embedded frontmatter and a sidecar.
3. **Orphan-check participation** — how sidecar-identified artifacts are counted
   in `ddx doc validate`.

## Pairing Rule

A sidecar is a YAML file whose name is the **full filename of the paired asset
with `.ddx.yaml` appended**:

```
foo.png          ←→  foo.png.ddx.yaml
diagram.excalidraw ←→ diagram.excalidraw.ddx.yaml
hero.svg         ←→  hero.svg.ddx.yaml
arch.pdf         ←→  arch.pdf.ddx.yaml
system-prompt.txt ←→ system-prompt.txt.ddx.yaml
```

Rules:

- The sidecar and its paired asset **must reside in the same directory**.
  A `foo.png.ddx.yaml` in a subdirectory does **not** pair with a `foo.png`
  in the parent directory, even if the paths are otherwise nearby.
- Sidecar filenames are **case-sensitive** on case-sensitive filesystems. The
  scanner must match exactly and must not normalize case.
- A `.ddx.yaml` file with no matching asset alongside it is a dangling sidecar.
  `ddx doc validate` reports it as an error (not just a warning), because it
  implies either the asset was deleted or the sidecar was incorrectly named.

### Why append, not replace

Appending `.ddx.yaml` to the full filename (including any existing extension)
preserves the original filename as an unambiguous prefix. Replacing the last
extension (`foo.png` → `foo.ddx.yaml`) creates ambiguity: a file named
`foo.ddx.yaml` alongside a `foo.png` would be misread as the sidecar of a
hypothetical `foo.ddx` asset, and files without extensions (`Makefile`,
`Dockerfile`) would produce bare `.ddx.yaml` sidecar names that glob patterns
cannot reliably distinguish from project-level config files.

### Glob pattern for discovery

The scanner uses the glob `**/*.ddx.yaml` to collect all sidecar candidates,
then strips the `.ddx.yaml` suffix to locate the expected paired asset.

## Sidecar File Schema

A sidecar is a YAML document (no frontmatter delimiters — it is pure YAML, not
markdown). The top-level key is `ddx:`, matching the frontmatter namespace used
in markdown artifacts.

### Minimal example

```yaml
ddx:
  id: DIAG-001
```

### Full example

```yaml
ddx:
  id: DIAG-001
  media_type: image/png
  depends_on:
    - FEAT-005
    - FEAT-007
  generated_by: SD-011
  inputs:
    - node:FEAT-005
  parking_lot: false
```

### Field reference

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `id` | string | **yes** | Artifact ID. Same prefix conventions as markdown artifacts (e.g., `DIAG-001`, `HERO-003`). Must be unique across the project graph. |
| `media_type` | string | no | IANA media type of the paired asset (e.g., `image/png`, `image/svg+xml`, `application/pdf`). When absent, DDx infers from the file extension. Explicit value overrides inference. |
| `depends_on` | []string | no | IDs of artifacts this one depends on. Creates `depends_on` edges in the document graph. Staleness cascades from these edges as with any artifact. |
| `generated_by` | string | no | ID of the artifact (typically a spec or design) that describes how this asset is produced. Creates a `generated_by` edge, which is **provenance-only** — it does not trigger staleness cascade (see Staleness below). |
| `inputs` | []string | no | Input selectors for prompt context resolution. Same selector syntax as markdown frontmatter. |
| `review` | object | no | Staleness tracking metadata (managed by `ddx doc stamp`). Same sub-fields as markdown: `self_hash`, `deps`, `reviewed_at`. The hash covers the paired asset bytes, not the sidecar YAML. |
| `parking_lot` | bool | no | When `true`, skip in staleness checks. Same semantics as markdown. |

Unknown fields within `ddx:` are preserved on round-trip.

### `generated_by` vs `depends_on` staleness semantics

| Edge type | Triggers staleness cascade? | Meaning |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------|
| `depends_on` | **Yes** | This artifact is governed by the upstream. If the upstream changes, this artifact may need updating. |
| `generated_by` | **No** | Provenance only. Records which spec or design describes the generator. Changing the generator spec does not automatically make the asset stale — regeneration is triggered explicitly via `ddx artifact regenerate`. |

This distinction mirrors the plan decision: "`generated_by` edge with separate
staleness rule (provenance, not authority — does not cascade like `depends_on`)."

## Conflict Rule

**Markdown files use embedded frontmatter exclusively. Non-markdown files use
sidecars exclusively. Mixing the two on a single asset is a validation error.**

The concrete conflict cases and their resolution:

| Situation | Outcome |
|-----------|---------|
| Markdown file with `ddx:` frontmatter; no sidecar alongside it | Normal markdown artifact. |
| Non-markdown file with a sidecar; no embedded YAML possible | Normal sidecar artifact. |
| Markdown file (`*.md`) with a `*.md.ddx.yaml` sidecar alongside it | **Validation error.** `ddx doc validate` reports: `<path>.md.ddx.yaml: sidecar alongside markdown file '<path>.md' — markdown artifacts declare identity in frontmatter; remove the sidecar`. The sidecar is ignored for graph construction to avoid duplicate IDs. |
| Two sidecars claim the same `id` as an existing markdown artifact | **Validation error.** Duplicate-ID check fires identically to the case where two markdown files share an ID. |
| A sidecar's `id` duplicates another sidecar's `id` | **Validation error.** Same duplicate-ID check. |

The prohibition on markdown+sidecar combination is strict because DDx's authority
rule is "identity present → artifact." Allowing both sources on a markdown file
would require a precedence rule, invite inconsistency, and add scanner complexity
for zero real-world benefit — markdown files can always carry their own identity
in frontmatter.

## Orphan-Check Participation

Sidecar-identified artifacts participate in `ddx doc validate` orphan detection
**identically to markdown artifacts**. No special treatment is applied.

An artifact is an orphan when it has no incoming edges (nothing depends on it)
**and** no outgoing edges (it depends on nothing). Sidecar artifacts with at
least one `depends_on` or one reverse-dependency edge from another artifact are
not orphaned.

### Common non-orphan patterns for sidecar artifacts

```
FEAT-005 ──depends_on──▶ DIAG-001   (FEAT-005 depends on the diagram)
DIAG-001 ──depends_on──▶ FEAT-007   (diagram depends on the doc-graph spec)
DIAG-001 ──generated_by─▶ SD-011    (provenance only — not an authority edge)
```

A `generated_by` edge **does count** toward orphan suppression: if DIAG-001
has only a `generated_by` link to SD-011, it is not considered orphaned, because
the provenance edge establishes that the asset is intentional and traceable.

### Dangling sidecar rule (distinct from orphan)

A `.ddx.yaml` file with no paired asset is **not** an orphan — it is a dangling
sidecar. These are reported separately from graph orphans:

```
ERROR: foo.png.ddx.yaml: sidecar has no paired asset 'foo.png' in the same directory
```

Dangling sidecars are hard errors (not warnings) because they indicate either
an accidental deletion or a copy-paste naming mistake, and they leave an artifact
ID in the graph that resolves to no file.

## Scanner Integration

The FEAT-007 scanner refactor must handle sidecars in the following order:

1. Glob `**/*.ddx.yaml` → collect sidecar candidates.
2. For each candidate, strip `.ddx.yaml` suffix → derive expected paired-asset path.
3. Check: does a file exist at that path in the same directory?
   - No → record dangling sidecar error; skip adding to graph.
   - Yes, and the paired asset is a `.md` file → record markdown+sidecar conflict error; skip.
   - Yes, and paired asset is non-markdown → parse YAML, extract `ddx:` block, add artifact to graph.
4. Continue normal markdown frontmatter scan for all `*.md` files.
5. Build unified graph from both sources. Apply duplicate-ID detection across both.

The scanner must not attempt to parse binary assets. It only reads the `.ddx.yaml`
sidecar file; the paired asset's bytes are only accessed when computing
`review.self_hash` during `ddx doc stamp`.

## Content Hashing for `ddx doc stamp`

When `ddx doc stamp` processes a sidecar artifact:

- `review.self_hash` is the SHA-256 of the **paired asset bytes** (not the sidecar YAML).
- The sidecar YAML is rewritten in-place with the updated `review` block.
- The hash covers the complete byte sequence of the paired asset as it exists on disk; no normalization is applied (images and binary files have no canonical form).

## Validation Summary

`ddx doc validate` checks on sidecar artifacts:

| Check | Severity | Message pattern |
|-------|----------|-----------------|
| Dangling sidecar (no paired asset) | error | `<sidecar>: sidecar has no paired asset '<asset>' in the same directory` |
| Sidecar alongside a markdown file | error | `<sidecar>: sidecar alongside markdown file '<md>' — use frontmatter instead` |
| Duplicate ID (sidecar vs. any artifact) | error | `duplicate id '<id>': found in '<path-1>' and '<path-2>'` |
| Sidecar with no `ddx.id` field | error | `<sidecar>: missing required field ddx.id` |
| Sidecar artifact with no graph edges (orphan) | warning | `<id>: artifact has no edges — add depends_on or ensure another artifact depends on it` |
| `depends_on` references unknown ID | warning | `<id>: unknown dependency '<ref-id>'` |
| `generated_by` references unknown ID | warning | `<id>: unknown generated_by target '<ref-id>'` |

data.text:
The commit landed cleanly. TD-025 covers all three design questions the bead required:

1. **Pairing rule** — sidecar is named `<full-filename>.ddx.yaml` (e.g., `foo.png` → `foo.png.ddx.yaml`), must live in the same directory, discovered via `**/*.ddx.yaml` glob.

2. **Conflict rule** — markdown files use embedded frontmatter exclusively; a sidecar alongside a `.md` file is a hard validation error. Duplicate IDs across any combination of sources are also errors.

3. **Orphan-check participation** — sidecar artifacts participate identically to markdown artifacts. Both `depends_on` and `generated_by` edges suppress orphan status. Dangling sidecars (no paired asset) are separate hard errors distinct from graph orphans.
